compiled_state.title: Migrate browser automation to @playwright/cli for sub-agent reliability
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## Context

DSO's design pipeline uses Playwright for live application review during wireframe creation (design-wireframe Phase 2). Currently this runs via temporary Bash scripts (`npx playwright`), which hits the ~73s Bash tool timeout for multi-step browser sessions. The alternative — Playwright MCP — is unreliable in custom sub-agents: multiple open issues (anthropics/claude-code#13605, #13898, #21560) document that plugin-defined sub-agents cannot access MCP tools and instead hallucinate results. Microsoft released `@playwright/cli` as a token-efficient alternative (~4x fewer tokens than MCP) designed for coding agents with filesystem access — short discrete commands (snapshot, click) that each complete under the timeout, with results saved to disk.

## Success Criteria

1. All DSO browser automation uses `@playwright/cli` commands via Bash instead of temporary Playwright scripts or MCP tool calls
2. Browser interactions complete within the ~73s Bash tool timeout (each command is discrete, not a multi-step script)
3. The `dso:playwright-debug` skill uses `@playwright/cli` for its browser interaction tier
4. Sub-agents dispatched via the Agent tool can perform browser automation without MCP access

## Dependencies

None

## Approach

Adopt `@playwright/cli` as the browser automation interface across DSO. Replace temporary multi-step Playwright scripts with discrete CLI commands (snapshot, click, fill, etc.) that save results to disk. Update design-wireframe's Phase 2 application review and playwright-debug's browser interaction tier to use the CLI pattern.

### Planning Intelligence Log

- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered — researched Playwright MCP access in sub-agents; found @playwright/cli as alternative; confirmed MCP inheritance bugs in custom sub-agents
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): not triggered
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none

comments.body:
## Revised Spec (Post-Brainstorm)

### Context (revised)
DSO operators running design pipeline and debugging workflows get silently wrong results when sub-agents attempt browser automation. Five skills (playwright-debug, debug-everything, validate-work, ui-discover, end-session) use Playwright MCP tools, but plugin-defined sub-agents cannot reliably access MCP — they hallucinate results instead of reporting tool unavailability (anthropics/claude-code#13605, #13898, #21560). Multi-step Bash scripts also hit the ~73s tool timeout. Microsoft's @playwright/cli (v0.1.1) provides discrete commands (~4x fewer tokens, disk-based output) designed for coding agents with filesystem access.

### Success Criteria (revised)
1. All 5 skills that reference Playwright use @playwright/cli commands via Bash instead of MCP tools, with the dependency pinned to @playwright/cli@0.1.1
2. A test suite of representative browser commands (navigate, screenshot, click, snapshot) each completes via a single Bash invocation within 60 seconds on a cold browser context
3. PLAYWRIGHT-MCP-GUIDE.md is removed and replaced by a CLI-focused guide documenting: (a) full command reference with examples, (b) disk-based output patterns, (c) command selection reference for low- vs high-fidelity needs
4. A regression test dispatches a sub-agent without MCP access and confirms browser automation works via @playwright/cli Bash commands
5. Session cleanup handles @playwright/cli state (orphaned processes, .playwright-cli/ temp directory) instead of .playwright-mcp/; browser binary management out of scope

### Scenario Analysis (18 surviving scenarios across 2 rounds)

**Critical:**
- Session persistence required: -s=name flag mandatory on every command, unique per skill+session+worktree
- Auth state must be per-session scoped to prevent credential bleed
- Pre-flight availability check (command -v playwright-cli) required to prevent hallucination fallback

**High:**
- Orphaned processes on ~73s timeout — timeout wrappers required
- Concurrent sub-agents need unique session names (include worktree name/PID)
- Browser binary download (npx playwright install chromium) part of setup
- Version pinning to @0.1.1 with integrity hash; npm version yanking risk requires documented fallback
- run-code as browser_wait_for substitute may not block on async mutations — spike must test
- ui-discover Python→CLI schema migration must verify field naming

**Medium:**
- Invoke CLI binary directly (not via npx) to avoid stdout pollution from npm warnings
- Validate output artifacts non-empty before proceeding
- Use absolute paths for CLI output
- Chromium revision mismatch between Python and CLI Playwright — spike must verify
- Sandbox/seccomp in CI may need --no-sandbox
- 60s timeout must account for slow CI runners

**Low:**
- Orphaned .playwright-cli/ dirs accumulate — pre-flight should clear stale sessions

### Research Findings
- All MCP tools have CLI equivalents; browser_wait_for via run-code workaround (unverified, spike required)
- willmarple/playwright-skill is a working reference implementation
- v0.1.1 is alpha with no stability guarantee — pin version, monitor for v1.0

### Approach (revised)
Begin with spike task to validate run-code as browser_wait_for substitute (including async mutation test) and confirm browser binary sharing (including Chromium revision compatibility). Pin to @playwright/cli@0.1.1. Use unique session names (-s=<skill>-<worktree>-<pid>). Invoke CLI binary directly to avoid npx stdout pollution. Replace all MCP tool calls across 5 skills. Rewrite guide. Update cleanup scripts.

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered — researched @playwright/cli command mapping, npm package details, MCP inheritance bugs, willmarple/playwright-skill reference implementation
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): re-triggered via gate — Round 1: 12 surviving scenarios; Round 2: 6 additional surviving scenarios (18 total)
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: scenario analysis re-run 1 time
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Adversarial review: 9 findings, 4 accepted. Full exchange: /tmp/workflow-plugin-bce9dedff503c924/adversarial-review-2f7a-7770.json
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Epic complete: all 7 stories closed, all 4 success criteria verified by completion-verifier (PASS), branch merged to main
